serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of mango green tea (approximately 350 ml / 350 g) as typically served at a Singaporean bubble tea or speciality tea stall.

nutrient_source.title: Mango Green Tea (No Topping) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for this item. Fat is reported as 0.0 g/100g by HPB; this is a confirmed zero by the approved source for this sugar-sweetened tea beverage formulation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mango green tea. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on the analogous food 'sugar-sweetened fruit-flavoured tea beverage' from the University of Sydney GI Database. The beverage contains 7.49 g sugar/100 g (predominantly sucrose/fructose from mango syrup) with zero dietary fibre, consistent with medium GI for liquid sugar-sweetened beverages. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (60 × 7.49 × 3.5) / 100 = 15.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (7.49 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 1 mg/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage, as regular consumption of added sugars at this concentration (7.49 g/100 g) has been independently associated with elevated blood pressure per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite the negligible sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.49 g sugar/100 g, zero dietary fibre, and medium GI (estimated 60); liquid sugars are absorbed rapidly causing acute postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely, as the 7.49 g/100 g sugar load with zero fibre and medium GI delivers rapidly absorbed liquid glucose that causes acute postprandial blood glucose spikes; plain unsweetened green tea is a suitable alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages at this sugar concentration is associated with increased CVD risk via triglyceride elevation and insulin resistance; fat and sodium are negligible.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage, as habitual intake of beverages containing ≥7 g sugar/100 g is linked to elevated triglycerides and increased CVD risk (AHA dietary guidelines), even though fat content is confirmed zero and sodium is negligible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (7.49 g/100 g) with zero fibre promotes systemic inflammation; no meaningful anti-inflammatory components present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage as the added sugar content promotes pro-inflammatory pathways; plain unsweetened green tea, which contains catechins and chlorogenic acids, is a preferable anti-inflammatory alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mango Green Tea (No Topping) is a popular Singaporean speciality tea beverage consisting of freshly brewed green tea combined with mango-flavoured syrup, served without any customisable toppings such as pearls, jelly, or pudding. The drink is light amber-green in colour with a sweet mango aroma and a mildly astringent green tea base. It is widely available at bubble tea shops and speciality tea stalls across Singapore. Per 100 g, it provides 29 kcal, 7.49 g carbohydrate (entirely as sugar from mango syrup), 0.01 g protein, 0.0 g fat (confirmed zero by HPB), and 0.0 g dietary fibre, with negligible sodium (1 mg). The complete absence of dietary fibre combined with the liquid sugar matrix means glucose is absorbed rapidly, making this a high-concern beverage for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 60 (medium) based on analogous sugar-sweetened fruit tea data from the University of Sydney GI Database, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 15.7 per standard 350 ml serving. Despite the beneficial catechin content of green tea, the added mango syrup negates anti-inflammatory benefits, making this unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis or cardiovascular disease risk. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and should be consumed only occasionally or replaced with unsweetened green tea.

confidence_reason: All per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with a reviewed status and confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source, and is clearly documented as an estimate.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero (0.0) per HPB Singapore Foodo database for this sugar-sweetened tea beverage formulation; this is a product where fat is absent by formulation. Saturated_fat_g and cholesterol_mg are also confirmed zero consistent with a fat-free beverage. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; this is a filtered liquid beverage with no fibre-contributing solids. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for this specific mango green tea formulation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. GI is an analogous estimate (60, medium) based on sugar-sweetened fruit-flavoured tea beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 26.215) / 100 = 15.7.
